stories.headline: Two children, ages 12 and 14, arrested in deadly Sunnyside neighborhood shooting
stories.summary:
Denver police have arrested two juveniles — a 14-year-old and a 12-year-old — in connection with the June 30 fatal shooting of a driver who confronted them while they were allegedly attempting to break into or steal his car near North Pecos Street and West 43rd Avenue in the Sunnyside neighborhood. Officers spotted the children in a stolen vehicle and arrested them following a short pursuit after they fled an attempted traffic stop. Both are being held on investigation of first-degree murder, with final charging decisions to be made by the Denver District Attorney's office.

Detectives also linked the 14-year-old to a separate shooting that occurred roughly 15 minutes before the homicide, in which multiple shots were fired at a juvenile victim near Park Avenue and East 20th Avenue. That victim survived, and two other juveniles were already charged in that case.

Investigators are additionally examining whether both suspects are connected to a homicide that occurred around 1:15 a.m. on June 24 near Olmsted Drive and Maxwell Place in Denver's Montbello neighborhood. The cluster of cases has raised serious concerns about juvenile violence across multiple Denver neighborhoods.

stories.headline: Denver police release body camera footage of officer shooting man charging with knife
stories.summary:
The Denver Police Department released body camera video Tuesday showing an officer shooting 26-year-old Wilson Guerra Bravo on July 5 near East 50th Avenue and Washington Street after Bravo allegedly charged the officer with a knife. Officers had initially responded around noon to reports of a man harassing people in the area.

Bravo was injured in the shooting and remains hospitalized. The release of body camera footage is part of Denver's standard protocol for officer-involved shootings and allows the public to review the circumstances surrounding the use of force.

The incident is under investigation. No additional details about the officer involved or Bravo's condition have been released.

stories.headline: Federal judge finds Denver officers conducted unconstitutional traffic stop of Black driver, forcing case dismissal
stories.summary:
A federal judge ruled that Denver police officers Emmett Hurd and Matthew Mullen conducted an unconstitutional traffic stop in May 2025 when they pulled over Azhaunte Forrest, a Black driver, citing stop signs that did not exist along his route and a taillight violation that did not hold up under state law. U.S. District Judge Nina Wang, ruling in January, found the officers lacked any legitimate basis for the stop and suppressed all evidence gathered, including an illegal firearm found under a seat, forcing federal prosecutors to dismiss the entire case against Forrest.

Forrest filed a civil lawsuit against the two officers Monday, alleging unlawful search and seizure. His attorney, David Lane, said the stop amounted to policing a Black man for driving a nice car and called for officer accountability.

The case drew attention to the use of pretextual traffic stops. The officers had pulled a U-turn to follow Forrest on Colfax Avenue after observing his taillights, then described his subsequent turns as evasive, even though they had not yet activated their lights or siren. Judge Wang found that the sole working red brake light on Forrest's BMW placed him in full compliance with Colorado law.

stories.headline: Second rescue in a week from abandoned missile silo near Arapahoe County Fairgrounds
stories.summary:
For the second time in seven days, emergency crews rescued people trapped in an abandoned missile silo near the Arapahoe County Fairgrounds after Arapahoe County Sheriff's deputies spotted two parked vehicles near a restricted property just after midnight on July 14 and heard voices coming from an underground shaft. A 19-year-old man and a 28-year-old woman were found approximately 60 feet below ground inside the shaft.

The man climbed out using a rope ladder, but the woman required assistance from Sable Altura Fire Rescue, which constructed a vertical rope rescue system to extract her by 1:45 a.m. Neither person was injured, though both received summonses for second-degree criminal trespassing.

The incident mirrors a July 8 rescue at the same silo, in which an 18-year-old woman and two teens became stuck underground. The Arapahoe County Sheriff's Office again warned the public against entering restricted properties or confined underground spaces, citing risks from unstable conditions, toxic gases, and deep vertical shafts.

stories.headline: EchoStar, one of Colorado's largest companies, files for bankruptcy protection
stories.summary:
EchoStar Corporation, one of Colorado's largest companies and parent of the Dish Network satellite television brand, has filed for bankruptcy protection after regulatory delays blocked the company from completing key asset sales needed to address its mounting debt load. The Englewood-based company has faced serious financial pressures in recent years as its satellite TV subscriber base eroded and it struggled to fund its wireless network ambitions.

The filing represents a significant moment for Colorado's business community, as EchoStar has long been one of the state's most prominent corporations and a major employer in the Denver metro area. The bankruptcy process will determine how the company restructures its obligations and what happens to its various assets and business lines.

Details on the specific bankruptcy chapter filed and the full scope of EchoStar's liabilities were not immediately available. The outcome of the proceedings will have implications for the company's workforce, creditors, and the broader Colorado technology and telecommunications sector.

stories.headline: Denver apartment recycling rules set for September launch, four years after voter mandate
stories.summary:
Denver plans to implement new mandatory recycling and composting rules on Sept. 1, following through on a 2022 ballot measure — Waste No More — that passed with more than 70% voter approval but took years to translate into enforceable policy. The new rules will, for the first time, require landlords of multifamily buildings with eight or more units to provide recycling access to residents, a change that will affect more than 100,000 Denverites who currently lack city-sponsored recycling pickup.

The ordinance also mandates that construction sites divert at least 50% of building waste from landfills, targeting what officials describe as a substantial and previously unaddressed waste stream. Denver's Office of Climate Action, Sustainability and Resilience described both provisions as potential game-changers for the city's waste diversion rates.

City officials said the first year of enforcement will focus on education and outreach rather than fines, giving landlords and business owners time to come into compliance. Advocates who backed the 2022 campaign welcomed the development but noted the lengthy delay, with some pointing out that residents had been requesting apartment recycling access for more than a decade.

stories.headline: Metro Denver housing market in 'constrained equilibrium' as fewer listings fail to spark demand
stories.summary:
Despite a significant drop in active home listings — down more than 21% year-over-year in June — Denver's housing market has not seen a corresponding surge in buyer demand, according to a new Colorado Association of Realtors report. Year-to-date home sales are up just 0.2% from last year, and the June median sale price was nearly flat at $650,000, reflecting a market that is stable but not particularly active.

One Denver County Realtor described the situation as "constrained equilibrium," in which low supply has helped support prices but has not restored the urgency that characterized the pandemic-era market. Homes are now taking an average of 39 days to sell, and approximately 12,088 properties were actively marketed at the end of June — nearly three times the inventory of June 2021 but still well below a traditionally balanced market.

A national trend toward relisting — where sellers pull homes off the market after 31 days and relist — is also playing out in Denver at an elevated rate of 3.1%, the 10th highest among major U.S. markets, according to Redfin. Analysts say the pattern reflects a mismatch between sellers holding onto pandemic-era price expectations and buyers constrained by high mortgage rates and affordability challenges.

stories.headline: Civil lawsuit filed against San Isabel Electric blaming utility for Aspen Acres Fire
stories.summary:
Four people have filed a civil lawsuit against the San Isabel Electric Association, alleging the rural electric cooperative is responsible for igniting the Aspen Acres Fire, which has burned nearly 100,000 acres in southern Colorado. The suit claims the utility's equipment or operations caused the fire and seeks damages for the destruction it caused.

The Aspen Acres Fire has been one of the most significant wildfires in Colorado this season, prompting evacuations and a disaster declaration from Gov. Jared Polis. The lawsuit marks an early legal effort to assign liability for the fire's damage to property, structures, and land across the affected region.

Utility-caused wildfire litigation has become increasingly common in the West as large fires result in significant property losses. The San Isabel Electric Association has not yet publicly responded to the suit's allegations.

stories.headline: Ozone Action Day Alert issued for Denver-Boulder Front Range corridor through Thursday
stories.summary:
The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment and the Regional Air Quality Council issued an Ozone Action Day Alert Wednesday afternoon for the Front Range Urban Corridor, stretching from Douglas County north to Larimer and Weld counties, including the Denver-Boulder area, Fort Collins, and Greeley. Authorities warned that sunny, hot weather will allow ground-level ozone to reach the "Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups" category on both Wednesday and Thursday.

Residents were advised to avoid rigorous outdoor activity during the heat of the day and to reduce vehicle trips where possible. Ground-level ozone can cause acute respiratory problems and trigger asthma attacks, and prolonged exposure can cause lasting lung damage.

The alert is compounded by ongoing wildfire smoke from multiple large fires burning across western Colorado, which continues to affect air quality across the region. The Ozone Action Day Alert was set to remain in effect until at least 4 p.m. Thursday, July 16.
stories.source: Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment

stories.headline: $1.8 million sculpture now illuminating Colorado Convention Center's new wing
stories.summary:
A major new public artwork, "Mountains and Rivers Without End" by California artist Cliff Garten, is now on display inside the Colorado Convention Center's western wing, suspended approximately 70 feet above the ground floor along a 145-foot span. The $1.8 million piece, which was approved more than five years ago as part of a $233 million convention center expansion, is visible from all levels of the building and can be seen at night from as far west as Speer Boulevard.

The sculpture is constructed from aluminum and glass and is based on a scale model of a 120-mile section of Rocky Mountain topography, with blue glass rivers running through aluminum mountain contours. It responds to natural and artificial light throughout the day, creating shifting shadows and profiles that echo views of the mountains visible from the lobby.

The piece joins other iconic works in Denver's public art collection, including "Mustang" at Denver International Airport and "The Big Blue Bear" at the convention center's 14th Street entrance. No formal opening event has yet been scheduled, as Denver Arts & Venues officials are looking for a fully clear weekend at the busy venue.

stories.headline: Park Burger to close longtime Highland neighborhood location at end of July
stories.summary:
Park Burger, a local burger mini-chain operated by Denver's Gastamo Group, will permanently close its location at 2643 W. 32nd Ave. in the Highland neighborhood on July 28 after more than 15 years at that address. The closure was prompted by the expiration of the restaurant's lease.

Gastamo Group founder and owner Philippe Failyau said the decision was not easy and expressed gratitude to the staff and community that supported the Highland location over the years. The company's two remaining Park Burger locations — the original in Platt Park and a second in Hilltop — will remain open.

Gastamo Group operates 14 metro-area restaurant concepts in total, including Homegrown Tap & Dough, Perdida, Lady Nomada, and Park & Co. The Highland closure adds to a recent pattern of long-running neighborhood restaurants in Denver losing leases as commercial real estate conditions evolve.

stories.headline: Avalanche sign forward Fabian Lysell to one-year deal for 2026-27 season
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche have signed forward Fabian Lysell to a one-year contract for the 2026-27 NHL season, adding depth to their forward group ahead of training camp. Lysell, a skilled winger, was a first-round pick known for his speed and offensive instincts.

The signing comes as the Avalanche manage a tight salary cap situation, with the team currently projected to be slightly over the cap ceiling with 23 players under contract. Colorado will need to make additional roster decisions before the season begins, particularly with star defenseman Cale Makar's contract extension on the horizon.

The Avalanche are coming off a successful 2025-26 campaign and are looking to build on that momentum. Lysell's addition gives the team another option along the wing as the organization navigates a busy offseason.

joke.joke: There are two kinds of people in this world: people who love pizza and people I don't understand.

topStory.headline: U.S. Expands Iran Strikes to Tehran Region, Fires on Blockade-Running Ship; Iran Retaliates Against Gulf Allies
topStory.summary:
The United States intensified its military campaign against Iran early Thursday, launching strikes that reached areas around the capital Tehran for the first time in the current round of fighting and firing on a vessel it accused of attempting to breach its reimposed naval blockade of Iranian ports. U.S. Central Command said the strikes, carried out over multiple waves, targeted Iranian command centers, air defense sites, missile and drone capabilities, coastal surveillance facilities, and cruise missile storage and launch sites, including on Greater Tunb Island in the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran retaliated before dawn, launching missiles and drones at U.S. allies in the region, striking Bahrain, Jordan, and Kuwait. Iranian military spokesperson Col. Ebrahim Zolfaghari warned that if the United States follows through on President Donald Trump's threats to strike Iranian bridges and power plants, Iran would retaliate by destroying regional infrastructure broadly, stating that the Strait of Hormuz represents Tehran's "invincible red line." Iran's top negotiator, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, told state media that Tehran had "no reason" to abide by the existing interim deal if it did not benefit from its terms.

The escalation comes after Washington reimposed a naval blockade on Iranian ports Wednesday, a move that reignited strikes and accelerated the unraveling of a preliminary agreement meant to pause the conflict that began when the U.S. and Israel launched military operations against Iran on Feb. 28. When that war started, Tehran closed the Strait of Hormuz to commercial shipping, causing the price of oil, fertilizer, and other goods to surge globally. Iranian officials said U.S. strikes since the latest round of fighting began have killed more than 35 people and wounded over 300.

Trump, speaking at the U.S. Army War College in Pennsylvania on Wednesday, suggested Iran was seeking a resolution but did not detail any concrete progress. "They want to settle so badly. They don't like what we're doing," he said, adding the U.S. would either reach a deal or "finish it off." Trump also acknowledged what he called a goodwill gesture from Tehran: the release of Dena Karari, a U.S.-Iranian citizen who runs a nonprofit and had been detained in Iran since late 2024 on espionage charges, according to her attorney, human rights lawyer Jared Genser.

Mediation efforts to calm the conflict have so far failed, and the exchange of strikes shows no sign of abating. Trump has threatened to broaden targets to Iranian civilian infrastructure, a move that previously drew condemnation from UN human rights chief Volker Türk, who said such strikes would constitute a war crime. With the interim deal in effective collapse, regional governments hosting U.S. forces now face direct Iranian retaliation, raising the risk of a broader regional war.
